name: Shu
description: Shu is a primordial Egyptian deity, representing the air, light, and the space between the earth and the sky. He is one of the first gods created by Atum, the self-created god, through either masturbation or spitting (accounts vary). Shu is often depicted as a man wearing an ostrich feather on his head, symbolizing lightness and emptiness. His primary role is to separate Nut (the sky goddess) and Geb (the earth god), who were initially locked in an eternal embrace. This separation allowed for the creation of the world and the flow of time. Without Shu, there would be no space for life to exist. He holds Nut aloft, preventing her from collapsing back onto Geb. Shu is also associated with Ma'at (truth, justice, and cosmic order), as his role in maintaining the separation ensures the continuation of the established order of the universe. He is crucial for the daily rebirth of the sun god Ra, ensuring the sun can rise and set. His children are Geb (earth) and Nut (sky). Shu's importance lies in his role as a divider and maintainer, preventing chaos and allowing the world to function according to cosmic principles. He embodies the vital element of air, which sustains life and provides the necessary space for existence.
subtitle: God of Air, Light, and Separation

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Shu
keyMyths.description: Shu was created by Atum, either through masturbation or spitting, marking the beginning of creation. This act established the first divine pair and set the stage for the emergence of the cosmos.

keyMyths.title: Separating Nut and Geb
keyMyths.description: Shu's most important task was to separate the sky goddess Nut from the earth god Geb, who were locked in a perpetual embrace. This allowed for the creation of space and the possibility of life.

keyMyths.title: Maintaining Cosmic Order
keyMyths.description: Shu's role in maintaining the separation between Nut and Geb ensures the continuation of cosmic order (Ma'at). Without his intervention, the universe would collapse into chaos.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
